data.title: Carpenter Technology: A Top Momentum Pick, But Looking Pricey Now
data.description:
Last month, I took notice of specialty metals and alloys manufacturer, Carpenter Technology (NYSE:CRS), after JPMorgan added the stock to its Analyst Focus List, saying the company is ''...positioned to benefit from strong pricing power in aerospace and industrial metals.'' Last year, I penned a bullish, contrarian piece on specialty contractor, MasTec, Inc. (MTZ), with my thesis hinging on the company's pricing power. Specifically, I referenced Goldman Sachs' interesting take that:

Unit labor costs have decelerated sharply and more quickly than inflation, supporting corporate profitability. Profit margins should also benefit from operating leverage in a solid economic growth environment. During periods of improving profitability, investors often reduce the scarcity premium assigned to strong pricing power stocks, and firms with less pricing power and more variable profit margins typically outperform."

Well, MTZ has outperformed, returning 85.5% since the time of publication vs. 17.7% gain by the S&P 500.

Carpenter stock broke out of a double-bottom base in early April after the company issued a positive FY 25 outlook, a bullish reversal pattern. CRS has emerged as a great momentum stock, having returned 38.9% in the year-to-date; 114.7% over the past 52 weeks and 1,045% over the past five years. Carpenter Technology makes specialized metals used in various industries, including aerospace, electricity generation, transportation and healthcare. For example, the company makes alloys for electric-vehicle motors, components for jet engines, turbine blades and discs for power generation and hardened metals for armored vehicles, among others. Its pivotal Specialty Alloys Operations segment comprises stainless-steel and premium alloy operations.

CRS is clearly benefiting from multi-year margin expansion and robust earnings growth. The company reported healthy Q3 Fiscal Year 2025 results a month ago. Carpenter reported a 58% jump in earnings to $1.88 per share, well above the highest analyst estimate, while operating income surged 81.5% to $137.8 million Y/Y. Sales climbed 6% to $727 million, $2.35 million below the consensus estimate. Carpenter's pivotal Specialty Alloys Operations segment delivered an adjusted operating margin of 29.1%, a big 770 bps improvement from last year's corresponding period.

The company upped its FY 2025 guidance, projecting full-year operating income to fall in the range of $520 million to $527 million, a nearly 50% Y/Y growth and well above the company's initial expectations. The company also expects adjusted free cash flow of $250 million to $300 million in fiscal year 2025. With ample free cash flow on its strong balance sheet, Carpenter is well positioned to not only sustain its current asset base but also to invest in growth initiatives and return cash to shareholders. For instance, the company intends to go ahead with its $400 million brownfield expansion project that encompasses a vacuum induction melting furnace and finishing assets targeted at the highest value applications and markets. These projects are expected to begin producing in FY28.

Diving into segment performance, Carpenter operates under Specialty Alloys Operations (SAO) and Performance Engineered Products (PEP). The company disaggregates revenue by diversified end-use markets and as well as by geographical locations. Aerospace and Defense is Carpenter's largest revenue segment, accounting for two thirds of corporate revenues. The segment posted revenue of $454.7 million, up 16.3% Y/Y; Medical revenue came in at $85.0 million, good for a -14.7% Y/Y decline, Energy related revenues clocked in at $46.4 million, up 19.9%, thanks to sales growth to the company's power generation customers. Transportation revenue was $28.1 million, an -18.3% Y/Y decline, Industrial and Consumer revenues contracted -7.3% to $90.5 million while Distribution revenues came in at $22.3 million, good for a -5.0% Y/Y decline. Overall SAO revenues were $642.9 million, good for 5.7% Y/Y growth, while PEP revenues increased 2.4% to $104.9 million. Meanwhile, the company revealed that orders were up 20% sequentially, highlighting the momentum in its business.

Looking at geographic performance, the United States is Carpenter's largest market, accounting for nearly 60% of corporate revenues while Europe is its second largest, bringing in ~22% of revenue. Last quarter, Carpenter recorded growth in two largest markets, with U.S. revenues jumping 13.1% Y/Y while revenue from Europe increased 13.4%. However, Asia Pacific, Canada and Mexico all recorded shrinking revenues.

Carpenter has projected operating income to expand at 25% CAGR through 2027 to hit $765 million to $800 million, outpacing most of its peers. The company's specialty alloy products for critical end-use applications not only command a significant premium but also provide significant barriers to entry. Carpenter's products are further upstream compared to those by aluminum peers Kaiser Aluminum Corporation (KALU) and Arsenal AIC Parent LLC. Carpenter has a greater focus on aerospace than both companies, especially after Kaiser's acquired the Warrick rolling mill, but has less exposure to packaging, hence Carpenter's higher margins.

That said, Carpenter says it expects earnings growth to continue beyond the forecast period of FY 2027, with productivity, volume and product mix all expected to continue to improve.

Aerospace and Defense Spending

Carpenter Technology counts the U.S. Department of Defense as one of its key customers, providing specialty alloys and engineered products for various defense applications. Recently, I penned a bullish piece on aerospace and defense solutions developer, M-tron Industries (MPTI), where I said '…defense stocks could emerge as some of its biggest winners thanks to its proposed $150 billion in extra defense spending in the current fiscal year, coupled with a report that the White House will request a record $1.01 trillion in national defense spending for FY26.''

Beyond the current administration, the diversity of Carpenter's aerospace and defense offerings positions the company for long-term A&D spend as airline companies replace their aging aircraft to support growing demand for air travel. ACI World has forecast passenger air traffic to double to 22.3 billion passengers by 2053, good for a CAGR of 3%.

Carpenter has signed several contracts with aerospace customers that include expanded share opportunities and favorable pricing. In addition, the company has a substantial backlog of around $2.0 billion, most of which is A&D related. High air travel demand is likely to result in higher repair, maintenance and overhaul demand, even in the event there's disruption in new plane builds.

Risks and Valuation

Carpenter does not appear to be facing any significant tariff threat, for now. The company sources its nickel--its largest raw material input - mainly from Canada. Thankfully, CEO Tony Thene revealed at the company's latest earnings call that Canadian nickel is exempt from tariffs. Further, Carpenter The company has established surcharge mechanisms that allow it to pass through higher input and raw materials costs to customers, albeit with some lag. According to Thene, there are few alternatives for the company's highly specialized products, with Carpenter being the only supplier in some cases.

By applying surcharges, Carpenter has also been able to largely mitigate exposure to volatile metal prices. The company's surcharge revenue has fluctuated between 13% and 28% of total revenue over the past decade, rising and falling according to metal price fluctuations.

The biggest drawback of this stock is the steep valuation. CRS has poor valuation scores, with most metrics including the PE ratio, EV/Sales, EV/EBITDA and Price/Sales suggesting the shares are significantly overvalued. However, its PEG of 0.98 is significantly lower than its peers, with the sector median at 1.48. This suggests that the market is assigning a premium valuation to CRS on expectations of continued superior earnings growth and profitability compared to its peers. Still, the lofty valuation could limit near-term gains.

The Bottom Line

CRS has built a lot of momentum thanks to multi-year margin expansion and robust earnings growth driven by the diversity of Carpenter's aerospace and defense offerings. Carpenter's products are designed to meet complex customer specifications, thus commanding a premium and driving higher margins. Further, the Trump administration is keen on ramping up defense spending, and Carpenter will likely reap the rewards.

Carpenter has guided for robust earnings growth over the next three financial years, which could partly support the high valuation of CRS. However, I think the market is assigning CRS a premium that's too high, leaving little margin for error or missteps such poor execution or earnings misses in the coming quarters. Carpenter ticks all the right boxes for an investor looking for long-term capital growth, but might not be a suitable candidate for buyers looking to cash in over the next 12 months. I rate CRS a Hold.

data.title: Amazon And Apple: Post-Earnings Updates To The Outlook
data.description:
Earnings recaps

Since reporting earnings recently, shares of Amazon.com Inc. (AMZN) and Apple Inc. (AAPL) have been volatile due to the shifting impact of the tariff exposure on their business outlooks. For both companies, the mixed results and guidance around the core businesses disappointed the market initially but has since seen expectations improve.

While the management commentary around the outlooks reinforced the uncertainty from the tariff overhang in earnings calls, President Trump’s recent trade deals with the UK and China ignited a relief rally in share prices. Questions remain about the outlook for the second half, especially the holiday season.

Amazon

Advertising, North America and International Retail business lines delivered solid results for the quarter, in line with expectations for revenues. While the International retail was in line with expectations at 3%, the 6.3% North America Retail margin was 20 bps below expectations of 6.5%, due to one-time charges around customer returns and costs to pull forward inventory ahead of the tariffs.

The company guided to Q2 total revenue of $159.0-164.0 billion, in line with consensus of $161.0 billion, and to an operating profit of $13.0-17.5 billion, which was slightly below the $17.7 billion expected. Since the release, the FY 2025 consensus total revenue has moved from $690.6 billion to $693.7 billion, due to the changing potential impact from tariffs.

Consensus revenue estimates for FY 2026 of $749.0 billion have moved up over $10 billion to $759.2, driven by higher revenue expectations in North America and International retail. The North America segment revenue is projected to move from $416 billion this year to $449 billion in FY 2026. Revenues from Advertising saw estimates slightly decreasing since last fall but now are expected to generate $65 billion this year and $75 billion next year, an increase of 16% year-over-year.

The North American retail operating margin for FY 2025 has declined from 7.2% last quarter to 6.6%. The Q4 holiday selling season margin estimate moved down from 8.5% at the end of last quarter to 7.8% currently. This margin decline is driven by a more conservative view of Amazon’s ability to reduce costs.

The International margin is expected to remain at 3% this year, but may be helped by the recent weaker dollar this year and longer term. Looking further out, consensus expects the North America margin to be 9%, down from 9.5% last quarter, and the International margin to increase to 5.3%, up from 5% last quarter, by the end of FY 2027.AWS delivered solid results last quarter. Coming into Q1, consensus was expecting $29.4 billion in revenue with an operating profit margin of 35%. While the AWS revenues were in line with expectations, the 39.5% AWS margin delivered a positive surprise, driven by lower costs for power and infrastructure.

For FY 2025, analysts are now expecting a 36.3% margin, up from 34.3% last quarter. On the earnings call, management highlighted the need for companies to move their data and infrastructure to the cloud to benefit fully from AI. To support Amazon’s growing need for technology infrastructure, CapEx is expected to increase further in FY 2025. CapEx is projected to increase in 2025 to $104.8 billion, up significantly from $52.7 billion in 2023.

The stock has been volatile. It is down 12.6% since the Q4 release but is up 9.7% since the Q1 release. The consensus P/E for FY 2026 is 29x, up from 22x ahead of the quarter.

Apple

The company delivered total revenues of $95.4 billion for Q2, in line with consensus, driven by $46.8 billion from iPhone. Greater China revenues were solid at $16.0 billion, in line with the $15.9 billion expected for Q2. The gross margins were mixed. The gross margin for Products was 35.9%, below consensus, and the gross margin for Services was 75.7%, ahead of expectations.

While the Q2 earnings results were solid, the commentary in the call around the outlook emphasized uncertainty. Apple guided for low to mid single-digit year-over-year revenue growth, on the low end of the 5% expected coming into the earnings release.

Consensus is now looking for 3% revenue growth or $88.8 billion for Q3. The gross margin was guided to 45.5%-46.5%, below the 47.1% initially expected. Consensus is now estimating at 46.1% gross margin, which incorporates a $900 million impact for tariff related costs. The company expects OpEx to be $15.3 billion to $15.5 billion, in line with consensus.

Overall total revenue estimates for FY 2025 and FY 2026 have been bouncing around since the January quarter. Over the past two weeks, revenues have bounced back up, especially for FY 2026, driven by the iPhone. The full-year iPhone revenue expectations have declined since January 2024 but have stabilized at between $202-203 billion, suggesting pessimism in the market about the potential upgrade cycle and the overhang from tariffs seems to be understood.

Expectations for the high-margin Services segment have remained stable for the second half, enabling the estimated total operating profit to edge up slightly to $128 billion for the FY 2025.

The company said that it continues to see increased customer engagement, with the Apple ecosystem supporting the future growth of the Services business. Based on consensus, Services are expected to hit $129.2 billion at the end of FY 2027, up $5.5 billion from the earnings release and close to revisiting the $130.5 billion estimated last quarter.

The stock has been volatile but has lost only 1% since the Q2 release. It is down 11% since the Q1 release. The consensus P/E for FY 2026 is 29x, up from 24x ahead of the quarter.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Capricor Therapeutics' Buy Thesis Ahead Of Deramiocel PDUFA Date
data.description:
Capricor Therapeutics, Inc. (CAPR) is a biotechnology company that advances two platforms. The first one is its cardiosphere-derived cell therapy called Deramiocel, and the second is its StealthX exosome engineering. Still, I consider its flagship candidate is Deramiocel, which has secured the FDA’s PDUFA date by August 31, 2025. Deramiocel’s BLA was based on robust Phase 2 and extension data. In fact, those trials showed a favorable safety profile and meaningful improvements in ejection fraction and upper-limb function in over 250 patients. And overall, I think CAPR’s compelling progress warrants a speculative “Buy” rating, especially since the stock still remains far well off from its previous 2024 highs.

Exosome Therapeutics And Rare Diseases

Capricor Therapeutics is a clinical‐stage biotechnology company developing cell and exosome-based therapeutics. In particular, CAPR believes this approach can help with the treatment and prevention of severe and rare diseases. Its initial focus was Duchenne muscular dystrophy [DMD]. They’re also headquartered in San Diego, California, and they were founded back in 2005. By November 2013, Capricor, Inc. merged with Nile Therapeutics and changed its name to Capricor Therapeutics, Inc., which trades under the ticker CAPR today.

Moreover, CAPR’s current roadmap goes back to its origin in Johns Hopkins, where researchers discovered they could influence heart repair with cardiosphere-derived cells [CDCs]. These cells work by secreting factors such as exosomes to modulate the immune response, reduce fibrosis, generate new blood-vessel growth, and eliminate inflammation.

This is why CAPR’s CDC-based therapeutic approach eventually led to Deramiocel, also known as CAP-1002. And interestingly, Deramiocel is also an off-the-shelf allogeneic CDC product aimed for commercial production in CAPR’s own GMP facility. In my view, this in-house production capacity could become a key strategic asset when CAPR transitions toward Deramiocel’s potential commercialization (contingent on the FDA’s green light).

It’s also worth mentioning that Deramiocel’s indication is for DMD. So far, trials suggest it can help preserve or improve muscle function, which in turn delays or slows down DMD’s progress in patients. More specifically, DMD’s cardiac manifestation of muscle degeneration is called cardiomyopathy, which is precisely what Deramiocel targets. Since treating cardiomyopathy avoids morbidity and mortality in DMD patients, it’s an overall potential treatment for DMD as a whole.

Having said that, CAPR’s pipeline actually includes one allogeneic CDC with Deramiocel, and multiple exosome projects for vaccines, gene, and drug delivery, and next-generation biologics. Aside from its main CDC platform, CAPR built an exosome engineering platform called StealthX that serves that purpose.

But still, as previously noted, Deramiocel is easily their most advanced candidate. It’s in Phase 3, and initially received a priority review. Eventually, Deramiocel’s BLA was accepted with a PDUFA date set by August 31, 2025. Its BLA was supported by their previous Phase 2 open-label extension trials. Also, CAPR partnered up with NS Pharma to further strengthen Deramiocel’s eventual rollout, which will support its US launch.

In fact, NS Pharma will also pay up to $80 million on FDA milestones, and up to $605 million in sales-based targets. There’s also the potential Priority Review Voucher [PRV] sale that could be worth around $150 million. But note that this PRV figure is based on prices at which other companies have sold their PRVs, so it’s just a referential value.

And as a last note, CAPR has three other candidates in its pipeline aside from Deramiocel. The exosome vaccine against SARS-CoV-2 was the first exosome program to reach IND-enabling and early-phase human work via National Institutes of Health [NIH] collaboration. In the preclinical stage, there are two additional exosome-based approaches, one for the delivery of therapeutic loads and one harnessing CDC-derived exosomes for DMD. But clearly, these candidates are still in relatively early stages, so I wouldn’t consider them major value drivers just yet.

Milestones And Market Potential

At the beginning of May, CAPR stock fell roughly 12% after the FDA ordered an advisory committee to discuss Deramiocel for DMD cardiomyopathy. After that, the company’s stock has recovered because CAPR believes this panel can showcase its positive data. Such data was produced with over 250 patients treated, for nearly five years, with promising efficacy and safety that could prompt Key Opinion Leaders [KOLs] and patient advocates to endorse its use.

But more importantly, DMD is a relatively large TAM for CAPR, with a prevalence of around 20,000 patients in the US and 200,000 worldwide. This suggests a global TAM by 2030 of approximately $27 billion. Unfortunately, DMD patients’ life expectancy is 25-30 years, mainly due to cardiac failure as its leading cause of death. And CAPR believes it could target its reimbursement prices at or above the current exon-skipping therapy costs.

Also, since CAPR already has the commercial and manufacturing infrastructure required to launch Deramiocel, it should be able to transition quickly into generating revenues if approved. On top of that, CAPR could soon be eligible for hundreds of millions of dollars in milestones from NS Pharma, plus the sale of a Priority Review Voucher. Still, it’s also worth mentioning that Deramiocel’s BLA was accepted solely for DMD-associated cardiomyopathy. This means that the drug is being reviewed only as a treatment for heart disease in DMD patients, which is a smaller subset of the broader DMD population.

Nonetheless, the company believes that the effects of its CDC approach might translate favorably into other therapies for non-DMD heart failure. After all, Deramiocel benefits injured heart tissue in general by reducing oxidative stress. What’s even more promising, Deramiocel even helps with heart inflammation, scar formation, and boosting mitochondria activity, which further stimulates muscle cell growth. Naturally, this broader potential for Deramiocel is still at the exploratory stage, but if they’re successful, it means Deramiocel could become a larger franchise over time. This is why CAPR also aims to sequentially expand into other patient groups and indications beyond cardiomyopathy.

For example, management believes they could target other rare heart diseases characterized by the same pathophysiology. This strategy would start tentatively with skeletal muscle myopathy in DMD, to protect upper-limb function. But over time, the next steps could involve Becker Muscular Dystrophy, which is a milder dystrophinopathy that mirrors that of Duchenne’s.

Plus, in theory, this approach would leverage CAPR’s previous trials on Deramiocel, so I think that at that point, its regulatory pathway should be even smoother by then. All it would need to do is supplement its expansion submission with Becker cohort data. In the long run, CAPR’s vision includes dystrophinopathy-specific labels for a true orphan cardiomyopathy therapy platform, which, I think, could easily become a major franchise.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, CAPR trades at a $503.2 million market cap, so it still seems relatively small compared to its broader potential in DMD. Also, its latest Q1 balance sheet shows it holds $28.8 million in cash and $116.0 million in marketable securities. This amounts to roughly $144.8 million in liquid resources against no financial debt other than regular operating liabilities. Similarly, its book value reached $127.7 million, indicating a P/B of 3.9. This is somewhat more expensive than its sector’s median P/B of 2.3.

Likewise, Seeking Alpha’s dashboard on CAPR projects that they’ll generate about $103.8 million in revenues in 2025. That prices CAPR at P/S of 4.9, but this is again higher than its peers’ P/S of just 3.3. So, overall, it would seem that CAPR trades at a slight premium, but I don’t think it’s excessively expensive. After all, DMD’s TAM of $27 billion alone could be more than enough to justify CAPR’s current valuation, assuming Deramiocel lives up to its promising. In my view, the upside potential is impressive in the bullish scenario. But naturally, this also brings us to CAPR’s main risk, which is that Deramiocel’s approval and commercialization may face unexpected delays and setbacks.

In fact, if the FDA were to reject Deramiocel altogether, that could very well invalidate my bull case on CAPR, and the shares would probably head much lower. A slightly less catastrophic scenario would be that Deramiocel is ultimately approved, but it takes much longer than anticipated, and its revenues also fail to live up to management’s lofty expectations. If this were to play out, the shares might trade sideways or slightly lower over time.

Even if Deramiocel is approved relatively quickly, its commercialization outcome remains a wildcard, as we’ll only know for sure once CAPR launches it. Also, I estimate CAPR’s current burn rate at approximately $7.5 million per quarter. I got that figure by adding its latest Q1 cash flows from operations and CAPEX. That suggests a very healthy runway of 19.3 quarters, or 4.8 years. But naturally, launching Deramiocel will probably increase its cash burn initially, and if the first sales figures disappoint, its runway could become an issue. And again, remember that if Deramiocel is approved, CAPR would probably be able to bolster its balance sheet by selling its PRV.

All in all, I think Deramiocel’s prospects are very promising if approved, as there are no other approved treatments for DMD’s cardiomyopathy. At most, I think it could compete indirectly with new gene therapies for DMD, but other than that, Deramiocel’s competitive profile looks compelling. And since the stock remains 49% off from its 2024 highs, I feel it’s a good time to start accumulating some shares overall.

Conclusion: Compelling Speculative “Buy”

Overall, I’m impressed by CAPR’s progress towards Deramiocel’s potential approval and launch in the near future. Naturally, its investment thesis remains highly speculative until we see a definite FDA green light for Deramiocel. But, based on my analysis, I feel that CAPR’s odds are looking favorable at this time. I also believe the upside potential of the bull case far outweighs the risks, so I rate CAPR a good speculative “Buy” for investors seeking exposure to a promising DMD-focused stock.

data.title: Monolithic Power Systems: Holding Steady, With AI Upside Still In Play
data.description:
I started covering Monolithic Power Systems (NASDAQ:MPWR) on Seeking Alpha back in January with a Buy rating. The thesis was straightforward: valuation multiples were hovering near historically low levels, and I had strong conviction in the company's business model and in management's ability to keep delivering consistent growth.

Investing in MPWR isn't exactly simple. On raw multiples alone, the stock almost always looks expensive. But that's because the company consistently proves it can target markets where its products bring real performance advantages over standard alternatives—and that kind of edge brings pricing power.

In my view, there are two ways to approach MPWR. One is to build a position gradually, trusting in the long-term quality. The other—the route I took last time—is to buy when valuation multiples dip. Those windows are rare and usually don't last long.

Sure, the market reminded us that low can go even lower. But in this case, sticking to the original thesis proved right.

Now, let's talk about the latest earnings.

Q1 2025 Earnings Call

MPWR's Q1 revenue came in at the top end of management's guidance, rising 39% year over year and 3% sequentially. Once again, I was impressed by the company's ability to leverage its core technology across multiple end markets.

While Enterprise Data saw a ~32% sequential decline (also down YoY), Storage and Computing and Automotive segments picked up the slack, allowing the company to post overall growth.

I attribute the drop in datacenter-related sales to a combination of revenue concentration, the naturally lumpy nature of these orders, and likely some share loss in NVIDIA's B200 platforms—where Infineon (OTCQX:IFNNY) appears to have gained ground, as reflected in their notably upbeat datacenter-related guidance for the year.

That said, management expressed confidence in regaining share in upcoming ramps, with new design wins already qualified and expected to scale in the second half. While they didn't name specific customers or platforms, I believe this refers to NVIDIA's B300, especially given the context and prior exposure. If that's the case, it suggests MPWR could recapture some of the share likely lost to Infineon earlier this cycle.

It's also worth highlighting the continued strength in automotive, especially in the face of a tough macro backdrop and rising tariff headwinds. This performance suggests that prior design wins are now converting into revenue, reinforcing the thesis that MPWR's specialization and high level of integration are still driving meaningful share gains.

They passed many rigorous testings and they feel good about it and we see so we feel good about it, we expect it to have a ramp. So at this time, we are not going to forecast what's the half year, in the past, we said - last year, we said that this year could be a flattish in our numbers. Let's see, let us deliver numbers hopefully, it's better than we expected.

It's also good to see that AI-related revenue is expected to diversify with the ramp of new entrants in the second half. This should help reduce concentration risk and smooth out some of the lumpiness we've seen in Enterprise Data. More importantly, it could also strengthen MPWR's positioning in its relationship with NVIDIA, giving the company more leverage as additional platforms come online.

So right now, the market has been dominated with really one main customer. And now as we see the second half of the year, in particular, there's going to be a number of new market entrants, and they're all with the same formula.

Besides, tariffs are not currently impacting costs or demand in a meaningful way. MPWR's long-standing diversification—across both R&D and manufacturing—seems to be working as intended, insulating the business from geopolitical friction.

I do believe there are some signs of demand being pulled forward in the Storage and Computing segment, but management noted that it's nothing particularly distortive. Even if there's some normalization ahead, AI-related ramps expected in the second half should help offset any softness in that segment.

If it happens, there's some pushing and pull out and pulling a pull out and a push out. And those are numbers small enough we don't clearly understand it, okay? And the overall numbers is very consistent.

All in all, I think everything is on track, with a real chance that AI-related revenue in the second half comes in better than expected.

Valuation

In terms of valuation, the stock has appreciated from around 36x forward earnings at the time of my last article to about 40x today. While that may still look expensive, it's actually in line with the company's historical average. For aggressive buying, I'd prefer to wait for lower entry points. That said, I do expect upside in the stock from potential earnings surprises, especially as new AI-related products begin to ramp in the second half.

If we look at recent revenue evolution, Enterprise has fallen from the levels it had at the end of last year, as we've discussed, a tendency I expect to be reverted, especially with the ramps in 2H. It represented 30% of revenues in the last twelve months but only 21% in the Q12025 quarter.

Automotive has been steadily increasing, showing a third quarter of double-digit growth. Going forward, I expect this trend to continue, especially with the adoption of 48-volt and 800-volt systems, although this revenue should mostly show off in 2026.

Storage and Computing had an exceptional Q1, with a 38% sequential jump that management attributed to genuine demand across memory and computing platforms—not just channel fill. While they didn't offer explicit guidance for Q2, the tone suggests they expect the segment to hold steady or normalize modestly. For the rest of the year, I think we'll see a push and pull between normalization from earlier pull-forward and incremental demand from AI-related server-side memory designs.

In other segments, I don't expect changes large enough to affect the scenario for the year, as they're smaller in share.

As per Seeking Alpha, markets are estimating an average of $2.66 billion in revenue for 2025. With Q1 already reported at $637.5 million and guidance for Q2 at $650 million, this implies about $1.37 billion in revenue for the second half of the year. That would represent an increase of just 6.5% from H2 over H1, which feels conservative considering the expected acceleration in automotive and enterprise-related growth.

I see a good chance of upward revenue revisions for the year, with Enterprise revenue expected to pick up, especially if Storage and Computing doesn't correct much—as management is currently guiding.

Risks

I'd say the main short-term risk for MPWR is the macroeconomic backdrop and the potential for a recession, given the stock's high beta and exposure to cyclical markets.

Another risk lies in market sentiment around the broader AI trade—particularly if concerns resurface around 2026 infrastructure investment. Investors are already watching closely for signs that big tech capex might have peaked, especially as spending runs high relative to free cash flow. In that sense, deals involving Saudi-backed initiatives are directionally positive, but it remains to be seen whether they'll translate into meaningful revenue.

Conclusion

MPWR continues to execute well, and its niche, system-level approach keeps driving gains across multiple end markets. Valuation-wise, multiples are now close to the company's historical average, so I wouldn't be aggressively adding at current levels—especially given the current U.S. macro environment.

That said, I do see upside risk to revenue estimates, particularly if AI-related demand reaccelerates. Either way, this remains a name I think you can own and hold for the long run, as the underlying thesis still stands strong.

data.title: Japan's Core Inflation Heats Up, Increasing July Rate Hike Odds
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang

Headline inflation stayed at 3.6% YoY in April for a second month

Japan’s headline consumer price index (CPI) remained at 3.6% for the second consecutive month, but core inflation, which excludes fresh food, came in hotter-than-expected. Core inflation jumped to 3.5% in April, the highest level since 2023, up from 3.2% in March. This exceeded the market consensus of 3.4%. Fresh food prices stabilised significantly (3.9% in April versus 18.8% in March). Broad-based inflationary pressures are building up, with price increases in housing (1.1%), medical services (2.3%), and recreation (2.7%). Energy prices also rose in April, mainly due to the end of government subsidy programmes.

Excluding both fresh food and energy, core-core inflation rose to 3.0%, suggesting that underlying inflation will remain above the BoJ's target of 2.0%. This should strengthen market expectations that the BoJ will hike policy rates sooner than expected. Our base case view on the BoJ is a rate hike in July. However, with US tariffs likely to impact manufacturing and exports negatively throughout this year, the BoJ's policy changes are likely to be gradual. Therefore, another rate hike will only be possible by early next year.

Core inflation is on the rise

Content Disclaimer

Original Post

data.title: XPeng: Competing Well, Hold For Now
data.description:
Introduction

Per my March article, XPeng (NYSE:XPEV) got off to a great start in 2025 for the first two months. We now have unit numbers for the first four months and XPeng is still hitting it out of the park.

My thesis is that XPeng is competing well against iconic companies such as Tesla (TSLA).

At the time of this writing, RMB 1,000 is about $139.

The Numbers

Looking at the CPCA Retail Sales Ranking Express Report for April 2025, XPeng is doing very well. In the first four months of 2024, XPeng retailed 28,283 units, which were only 17% of Tesla's 163,841 units. Things are different now! In the first four months of 2025, XPeng retailed 117,736 units, which were 72% of Tesla's 163,338 units. These numbers are specific to China, but it is only a matter of time before the companies who are winning in China start winning in the rest of the world:

CnEVPost shows strong deliveries of XPeng's Mona M03 model. It has been hovering at about 15 thousand monthly units since December:

The P7+ and G6 models are not as popular as the M03, but they still make contributions. The G9 and X9 models don't move the unit needle as much as these others.

Valuation

A key valuation consideration for auto companies is their ability to sell outside of their home market. XPeng exported 23,409 vehicles in 2024 and the top countries were Israel, Denmark and Norway. Per a recent Financial Times article, Chinese EVs are selling well in Brazil, Thailand and Mexico. I expect Chinese vehicles from companies like XPeng to increase sales outside of China dramatically in the years ahead:

The companies participating in smart-driving and self-driving have many opportunities to see their valuations change dramatically. Waymo (GOOG) (GOOGL) has a fleet of 1,500 robotaxis and they have given 10 million rides. Apollo Go (BIDU) has given 11 million rides, and they have a fleet of 1,000 vehicles. Per XPeng's April vehicle delivery announcement, their Xpeng Navigation Guided Pilot ("XNGP") system has a high penetration rate, and they are using their technology to offer insurance:

In addition to robust delivery growth, XNGP achieved a monthly active user penetration rate of 84% in urban driving in April 2025. On April 28, 2025, XPENG officially launched its ADAS Insurance Service in China. Priced at RMB 239 per year, this service offers additional coverage when NGP is in operation and is made available to all XPENG models through partnership with leading insurance providers in China.

Per the 1Q25 call, the vehicle gross margin has improved for seven straight quarters. Looking at the 1Q25 release and the 2024 annual report, trailing twelve month ("TTM") operating losses were RMB 6,053 million [RMB 1,041 million + RMB 6,658 million - RMB 1,646 million]. This came from TTM gross profit of RMB 7,462 million [RMB 2,460 million + RMB 5,846 million - RMB 844 million] and TTM revenue of RMB 50,129 million [RMB 15,811 million + RMB 40,866 million - RMB 6,548 million]. It isn't out of the question for optimistic investors to value XPeng at 3x TTM revenue +/- 10% which is about RMB 180,464 million to RMB 220,568 million. Translating to USD, this is about $19 to $23 billion.

The 1Q25 release says the weighted average number of ADS units is 949,682,796. Multiplying by the May 21 ADS price of $22.25 gives us a market cap of $21 billion. The market cap is inside my valuation range and I think the stock is a hold right now.

XPeng can never be accused of stasis as they are always preparing for the future. Forward-looking investors should keep tabs on the public debut of the G7 model in June. Another upcoming development investors should watch is the fifth-generation Iron robot, which will be powered by Turing chips. And, of course, investors should monitor the financials closely to see when XPeng has enough scale to turn from an operating loss to an operating profit.

Disclaimer: Any material in this article should not be relied on as a formal investment recommendation. Never buy a stock without doing your own thorough research.

data.title: Nu Holdings: A Top Fintech Play For 2025
data.description:
Nu Holdings (NYSE:NU) reported decent earnings for its first fiscal quarter last week amid strong growth momentum in the Fintech's core markets in Brazil, Mexico and Colombia. In the first-quarter, Nu Holdings reported strong customer acquisition momentum and the Fintech improved its customer monetization as well, mainly because customers increase their platform spending over time. In my opinion, Nu Holdings is a well-run and highly promising Fintech enterprise with an improving profitability profile, an attractive growth run-way in Latin America and upside potential in the valuation.

Previous Rating

I recommended shares of Nu Holdings in my last work on the Fintech in March, mainly because the financial services company made progress in growing its profitability while at the same time maintaining strong growth. In the first-quarter, Nu Holdings continued to excel in its core markets and benefited from a growing deposit base. Shares of Nu Holdings are undervalued given the Fintech's growth potential, and I continue to recommend the financial services platform as a buy.

Nu Holdings is Seeing Massive Customer Acquisition Momentum

Nu Holdings met EPS estimates for Q1'25 and beat top-line estimates. The Fintech reported adjusted earnings per share of $0.12 which met Wall Street's expectation. The Fintech's revenues came in at $3.25B, which resulted in a $20.8M consensus estimate beat.

Nu Holdings is a Latin America-based Fintech platform with operations mainly in Brazil, Mexico and Columbia, which represent the company's core markets. Nu Holdings is especially popular in Brazil, where about 59% of the adult population owns an account on the Fintech platform. At its core, Nu Holdings is a digital banking platform that offers customers financial products that allow them to save, invest and borrow money. Loans and credit card products make up an especially large portion of the Fintech's product portfolio.

The single biggest reason to own Nu Holdings is to participate in the company's massive customer growth. In the first-quarter, Nu Holdings grew its customer base by ~19% year-over-year to a massive 119 million. In the last twelve months, the Fintech acquired more than 19 million new customers who are now participating in its financial ecosystem. About 104 million of those 119 million were customers from Brazil, representing a customer share of 88%.

Nu Holdings' operational success is not only measured by its growing customer count, but also by its ability to attract deposits from its customers. Like any good bank, a growing deposit base is key to also selling more loans and insurance products on the platform. In the first-quarter of FY 2025, Nu Holdings generated a massive 48% growth (on currency-neutral terms) in its deposit base to $31.6B. Brazil represented 77% of the Fintech's deposit base in Q1'25, highlighting the importance of this specific market for the company's future.

One key aspect that stood out from Nu Holdings' first-quarter earnings report card on May 13, 2025, was that the Fintech maintained not only its customer acquisition momentum, but also managed to improve customer monetization.

Customer monetization is a key figure for fast-growing Fintechs because it shows how well a financial services platform is growing its organic platform spend. Similar to growing dollar-based net retention rates for software companies that operate on the same principle of pursuing scale over short-term profitability, Fintechs need to grow their customers' platform spending over time.

This organic revenue growth gets quantified by a figure called 'ARPAC' which stands for 'average revenue per active customer'. In the March quarter, Nu Holdings generated $25.90 per active customer in revenue for customers that were already on the platform for eight years. Over time, customers tend to ramp up their spending as they use the platform's various products, which means that customers who stay with Nu Holdings for longer are obviously the Fintech's most profitable customers. The trend in ARPAC is showing strong growth and if Nu Holdings can maintain this trend, I believe the Fintech is facing very favorable profitability tailwinds in the years ahead.

Gross Profit Trajectory Highlights Solid Profitability

Fintechs can grow into much higher profitability if they can successfully scale their operations and maintain acquisition momentum. In the first-quarter, Nu Holdings has been widely profitable with a total gross profit of $1.3B, showing 32% year-over-year growth (currency-neutral). With this kind of profitability, Nu Holdings has the potential to use cash from its operations to either acquire other Fintech start-ups, or, more likely, go on an aggressive acquisition spree in other countries in Latin America. Mexico still represents a top growth opportunity for the Fintech, as only 17% of deposits come from this rapidly growing country.

Nu Holdings' Valuation

Shares of Nu Holdings are comparatively cheap given the potential for the Fintech in Latin America and its proven growth achievements in the last several years. The Fintech is priced at 16.5X forward (FY 2026) earnings, which is about 6% above its longer term, 3-year average P/E ratio of 17.5X.

SoFi Technologies (SOFI), Block (XYZ) and Upstart Holdings (UPST)—which are all U.S.-based Fintech platforms—are trading at forward price-to-earnings ratios of 28.4X, 14.3X and 21.3X. The industry group average P/E is 20X, which is a valuation that I can definitely see Nu Holdings reprice to if the Fintech maintains its customer and gross profit growth. I recently recommended shares of Block, a Fintech that has solid momentum for its Cash App ecosystem: I Have Been Waiting For This Drop.

I believe a 20X P/E ratio for Nu Holdings, given its profitability and growth profile, is achievable, which implies a fair value in the neighborhood of $15.52 per share, showing 24% upside revaluation potential.

Risks With Nu Holdings

The biggest risk for Nu Holdings, as I see it, relates to a potential slowdown in its customer acquisition momentum in Brazil because this market is clearly key to backing up the Fintech's business. Nu Holdings has steadily grown its customer base since its inception, however, and since the Fintech is already profitable, I believe the risk matrix actually favors investors here. What could get me to change my mind about Nu Holdings (and potentially my stock rating) is if the financial services platform were to see slowing customer acquisition growth in its core market, Brazil or if monetization figures like ARPAC weakened.

Final Thoughts

There's a lot to like about Nu Holdings, especially if you are willing to venture outside of the North American market to find and invest in a promising Fintech brand. Nu Holdings is growing rapidly and has a very strong market position, especially in Brazil. The Fintech is making massive progress in terms of growing its loans, deposits and profitability, through improving customer monetization as well as expanding its platform reach. Shares of Nu Holdings are very attractively valued, especially in direct comparison to U.S.-based Fintech enterprises: Nu Holdings is valued at a 16.5X earnings multiplier and therefore trades about 23% below the valuations of comparable U.S. Fintechs. I see a very profitable risk profile for shares of Nu Holdings going forward, and would not be surprised to see aggressive expansion plans by the Fintech in the future.

data.title: Mastercard: The Long-Term Compounding Train Marches On
data.description:
From quarter to quarter, Mastercard (NYSE:MA) proves the doubters wrong, as it maintains mid-teens growth rates with enviable margins.

I argue that Mastercard (or Visa) are a must-have in a long-term portfolio, as the market continues to underestimate their dominance, and significant runway for continued growth.

Let's dive in.

Introduction

I've been writing about Mastercard and Visa here on Seeking Alpha since 2023, maintaining a bullish view throughout the period.

Underlying my thesis is that these are two companies that are exceptionally managed, extremely profitable, with essentially an infinite runway for above-market growth.

Considering the above, at least one of them is almost a must-have in a long-term portfolio. Occasionally, the market provides great buying opportunities into these names, typically driven by worries of some form of regulatory disruption, and sometimes because of worries about a spending slowdown.

Such was the case in late 2022, the majority of 2024, and again as recent as the early part of this year. That said, these types of long-term compounders turn into market-beating investments over time, even when bought at a fair (rather than cheap) valuation.

This is just the power of compounding. And still, many investors argue that there is no alpha to be made with Mastercard and Visa. That they are already too big and known, and that up-and-coming companies like Adyen (OTCPK:ADYEY) and Stripe are set to disrupt them.

Here's what they are missing.

The Key Metrics to Monitor in Mastercard's Earnings Continue to Improve

In the first quarter of 2025, Mastercard's revenue growth accelerated to 14.2%, reaching sales of $7.3 billion. Growth was led by Value-Added Services, which rose 16% to $2.8 billion, followed by Payment Network, rising 13% to $4.4 billion, net of incentives.

Amid worries of a spending slowdown and a maturation of the cash-to-card runway, Mastercard showed strong volume growth, with Worldwide volumes growing 9%, United States growing 7%, and International growing 10%, all far ahead of PCE.

As Mastercard adds more and more lines of business in VAS, some investors are concerned about deterioration in margins, as the payment network business is arguably one of the most profitable businesses in history. And yet, Mastercard's margins and operational leverage show no signs of slowing down.

Looking ahead, Mastercard continues to expect low-double digit revenue growth, on high-single digit operating expenses growth, reflecting the continuation of the historical tempo.

So, all in all, it seems like nothing is slowing the Mastercard train, despite the coming-and-going worries about macro, competition, and maturation.

Mastercard and Visa Are Borderline Unbreakable, But Who is Doing Better?

One of the constant themes of my analyses on the companies over the years was that Visa is underestimated compared to Mastercard, with the latter trading at a significant premium.

This premium is justified by Mastercard's faster growth, but I argue it should be smaller, in light of Visa's higher margins, and the fact that over time I expect the growth gap to close.

In Q1 of 2025, Mastercard's total volumes grew 9.3%, compared to Visa's 6.7%, marking the smallest since 2023, as Mastercard laps large customer wins it took from Visa.

While on the subject of customer wins, we can see that Mastercard pays out a far greater portion of its gross revenue to clients as incentives, which is one of the key reasons for its ability to win some business from Visa. This is reflected in the companies' respective margins, as Visa's operating margins are about 8–10% higher.

And while most investors and analysts focus on the old payment network business, where Mastercard is growing faster, I argue that much more attention should be direct to Value-Added Services, and New Flows.

The consumer payments business should continue to grow at a high-single-digit pace for years to come, but the other business lines should grow in the high-teens and even low-twenties. Over time, both Visa and Mastercard expect the non-consumer segments to become about half of their revenues, and that's meaningful.

As noted above, Visa is slowly closing the gap from Mastercard, and has achieved a 52% share of their combined revenues from VAS, which is now at a combined run rate of over $21 billion.

Last quarter, Visa grew 22% compared to Mastercard's 16%, and over time this should be the primary driver for the growth gap narrowing.

Valuation

At $569 a share, Mastercard trades at a 36x forward P/E, compared to Visa's 31x, reflecting a 16% premium. This compares to the average 20% premium over the past five years. So, we're seeing a slight narrowing there, which is the main reason for Visa's outperformance over the past three years.

Looking ahead, Visa is expected to grow EPS at a 12% rate, on 10% growth in revenues, while Mastercard is expected to grow EPS at a 15% rate, on 12% revenue growth, though 2025 should be a slower year for Mastercard due to higher tax rates.

As notable from the PEG graph above, which normalized the P/E based on growth, Mastercard's premium is finally at the right spot, with both companies trading at the same valuation based on 1-year-out numbers.

Conclusion

Mastercard, and its arch nemesis in Visa, continue to be underestimated by the market, which values them in line with the market average when accounting for their growth.

I still have a slight preference for Visa, qualitatively, because I believe its larger size supports continued outperformance in the non-consumer lines. However, I believe both companies should perform hand in hand for the foreseeable future.

And, at around 2x PEG, both remain in line with market-average valuations, despite being far higher in terms of quality, which means I think both Mastercard and Visa are a good investment at these levels.

As such, I reiterate Mastercard at a 'Buy'.

data.title: Above The Noise: Making Sense Of Markets, Treasuries, And Tariffs
data.description:
By Brian Levitt, Global Market Strategist

“If every instinct you have is wrong, then the opposite would have to be right.”

Jerry Seinfeld’s advice to George Costanza has felt relevant to investors lately. But unlike Costanza’s decision to order chicken salad on untoasted rye, the smarter move for investors may have been to do nothing at all. Just as $74 billion flowed out of equity mutual funds and ETFs,1 the market bounced back into positive territory for the year.2 Cursed again by our action biases!

To be fair, the fundamentals haven’t shifted much. Tariffs remain elevated,3 and business sentiment is still historically weak.4 Yet markets have rallied,5 likely because we’ve moved past peak policy uncertainty.6 There are signs that certain market pain points are forcing the administration to soften its more extreme trade stance.

Looking ahead, do markets need full clarity on trade to keep rising, or just incremental progress? Recent history favors the latter. In December 2018, President Trump agreed to a 90-day trade truce with China. The phase one deal wasn’t signed until January 2020, but the market performed quite well in the interim.7

Serenity now!

It may be confirmation bias, but…

…rumors of the demise of demand for US Treasuries have been greatly exaggerated in my opinion. Markets absorbed May’s Treasury auction, with little evidence to support fears of foreign Treasury boycotts. In fact, the average 10-year US Treasury note bid-to-cover ratio over the past three months is slightly above the long-term average.8 I’d expect US Treasuries to continue to trade based on expectations of monetary policy and nominal growth, and not on concerns of fiscal sustainability.

Since you asked

Q: Could the new tariffs generate enough revenue to meaningfully change the US fiscal picture?

A: It’s unlikely. But let’s quantify it:

  • In 2024, the US government collected approximately $98 billion in customs and excise taxes, accounting for about 2% of the total $4.9 trillion in tax revenue.9
  • If the effective tariff rate were to rise to 10%, that would represent a fourfold increase over the 2024 level.10 Assuming all other factors remain constant, customs tax revenue in 2025 could reach $400 billion, or roughly 8% of last year’s total tax receipts.
  • All else may not remain equal, however. A higher tariff rate would likely reduce import volumes because demand could decline, and consumers might shift toward domestically produced or alternative goods.

It was said (part 1)

“This one-notch downgrade on our 21-notch rating scale reflects the increase over more than a decade in government debt and interest payment ratios to levels that are significantly higher than similarly rated sovereigns.” – Moody’s Investors Service

Moody’s Investors Service downgraded US government debt on May 16, echoing similar past actions. Standard & Poor’s did so 14 years ago, and Fitch two years ago.11 In short, this isn’t new.

The current downgrades, like those before them, don’t pose a structural threat to the US Treasury market. US Treasuries are their own asset class and thus are insulated from forced selling. It’s also important to remember that the US is an exceptionally wealthy nation. While government debt stands at approximately $37 trillion,12 total US household net worth is nearly $160 trillion.13

There are also viable policy options available to help curb the growth of the national debt. Ultimately, it’s a question of political will.

It was said (part 2)

“Now, if the chairman of the Fed ... if he would lower interest rates like China did, like I think the UK did, but like numerous other countries have done, it would be, it’s like jet fuel ... but he doesn’t want to do it… Probably he’s not, he’s not in love with me.” – President Donald Trump

It’s unlikely that Federal Reserve (Fed) Chairman Jerome Powell’s personal views toward the president have any bearing on Fed policy. It currently finds itself in a difficult position: Tariffs are expected to slow economic growth while simultaneously pushing prices higher, at least in the short term.

Meanwhile, as the US faces potential shortages and rising prices, China and countries in Europe may be dealing with the opposite problem: An oversupply of goods originally intended for export. For the US, the challenge is near-term inflation; for much of the rest of the world, it could be near-term deflation. That contrast helps explain why central banks globally are shifting toward more accommodative policies.

My suggestion: Don’t fight the European Central Bank or the People’s Bank of China.

Our Portfolio Playbook says

The answers to three questions help guide our investment views in our Portfolio Playbook.

1. Where are we in the cycle?

A recession doesn’t appear imminent. If it were, credit spreads would likely be significantly wider.14

2. What’s the direction of the economy?

Global growth is expected to remain below trend and continue to deteriorate.15

3. What are the policy implications?

Policy easing is ongoing, with more aggressive measures being implemented outside the US.

Given this backdrop, a prudent investment approach could be to focus on improving portfolio quality and reducing valuation. Diversifying beyond mega-cap US growth stocks into value, mid-cap, and international stocks may help reduce overall portfolio valuations.16 A lower-valuation portfolio may offer downside protection and may be well-positioned to benefit from a potential global economic recovery.

Everyone has a podcast

For each episode of our Greater Possibilities podcast, we phone a friend to learn more about a topic. In the latest episode, we phoned Justin Leverenz of the Invesco Developing Market team. Here’s what he said:

  • The performance of emerging market stocks — both absolute performance in US dollars and relative performance to US stocks — depends primarily on two factors: China and the US dollar. We contend that we may be at the inflection point where both align to be positive forces on returns.
  • Global growth may inevitably shift towards Asia and Europe over the next few years. Both geographies have considerable fiscal policy latitude to reflate domestic demand, given the shock of weaker exports.
  • The case for Chinese stocks hinges on a recovery in domestic demand and a shift away from excess savings towards domestic consumption. The Chinese government has already taken steps in this direction, implementing measures to boost domestic demand and reduce reliance on exports.

On the road again

My travels took me to a conference in Atlanta, where the keynote speaker was Dominique Wilkins. It’s not every day that I get to hear from an NBA legend and Hall of Famer. He focused on the value of healthy competition. He admitted that he hated the competition when he played, but he still looked up to the league’s best players. Competition can make you better. It also makes the game more fun.

Footnotes

  1. Source: Investment Company Institute, May 7, 2025. Latest data available. Mutual fund data represent estimates of net new cash flow, which is new sales minus redemptions combined with net exchanges, while exchange-traded fund (ETF) data represent net issuance, which is gross issuance less gross redemptions. Data for mutual funds that invest primarily in other mutual funds and ETFs that invest primarily in other ETFs were excluded from the series.

  2. Source: Bloomberg L.P., May 19, 2025. The year-to-date total return of the S&P 500 Index as of market close on May 16, 2025, was 1.80%.

  3. Source: United States International Trade Commission, May 2025.

  4. Source: CEO Executive Magazine, April 30, 2025. The CEO Confidence Index is a monthly survey of chief executives. Each month, Chief Executive Magazine surveys CEOs across corporate America at organizations of all types and sizes to compile and create an index.

  5. Source: Bloomberg L.P., May 19, 2025. Based on the return of the S&P 500 Index since the recent market trough on April 8, 2025.

  6. Source: Baker, Bloom, & Davis, May 18, 2025. Based on the US Economic Policy Uncertainty Index, a widely used metric to quantify economic uncertainty related to policy decisions. It hit a 2025 peak on April 5, 2025.

  7. Source: Bloomberg L.P., May 19, 2025. The S&P 500 Index returned 31.5% in 2019. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

  8. Source: US Treasury, May 6, 2025. Latest data available. The bid-to-cover ratio measures the total amount of tenders and the total amount of Treasury accepted bids on a competitive basis. Generally, this number is positively correlated to demand (the larger the number, the greater the demand).

  9. Source: US Treasury, Dec. 31, 2024.

  10. Source: United States International Trade Commission, May 2025.

  11. Source: Standard & Poor’s, Fitch Ratings.

  12. Source: US Treasury, Dec. 31, 2024.

  13. Source: US Federal Reserve, Dec.31, 2024.

  14. Source: Bloomberg L.P., May 19, 2025. Based on the option-adjusted spread of the Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield Bond Index, which measures the US dollar-denominated, high yield, fixed-rate corporate bond market. Securities are classified as high yield if the middle rating of Moody’s, Fitch, and S&P is Ba1/BB+/BB+ or below. Option-adjusted spread (OAS) is the measurement of the spread of a fixed-income security rate and the risk-free rate of return, adjusted to account for an embedded option, such as calling back or redeeming the issue early. The Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield OAS on May 19, 2025, is 303 basis points compared to a long-term average of 498 basis points.

  15. Sources: Bloomberg L.P., MSCI, FTSE, Barclays, JPMorgan, Invesco Solutions research and calculations, from Sept. 1, 1992, to April 30, 2025. The Global Leading Economic Indicator (LEI) is a proprietary, forward-looking measure of The Global Leading Economic Indicator (LEI) is a proprietary, forward-looking measure of the growth level in the economy.

  16. Source: Bloomberg L.P., April 30, 2025. Based on the price-to-earnings ratio of the S&P 500 Index (23.8x) compared to other indexes such as MSCI Europe Index (14.7x), Russell 1000 Value Index (18.5x), and Russell Midcap Index (20.4x).

Important information

NA4519401

All investing involves risk, including the risk of loss.

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Investments cannot be made directly in an index.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

Fixed income investments are subject to credit risk of the issuer and the effects of changing interest rates. Interest rate risk refers to the risk that bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise and vice versa. An issuer may be unable to meet interest and/or principal payments, thereby causing its instruments to decrease in value and lowering the issuer’s credit rating.

Investments in companies located or operating in Greater China are subject to the following risks: nationalization, expropriation, or confiscation of property, difficulty in obtaining and/or enforcing judgments, alteration or discontinuation of economic reforms, military conflicts, and China’s dependency on the economies of other Asian countries, many of which are developing countries.

Credit spread is the difference in yield between bonds of similar maturity but with different credit quality.

Diversification does not guarantee a profit or eliminate the risk of loss.

Inflation is the rate at which the general price level for goods and services is increasing.

Monetary easing refers to the lowering of interest rates and deposit ratios by central banks.

The MSCI Europe Index captures large- and mid-cap representation across a universe of developed market countries in Europe.

An overnight index swap (OIS) is an interest rate swap involving the overnight rate being exchanged for a fixed interest rate. An overnight index swap uses an overnight rate index, such as the overnight federal funds rate, as the underlying rate for its floating leg, while the fixed leg would be set at an assumed rate.

The University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index is published monthly, based on a telephone survey designed to assess US consumer expectations for the economy and their personal spending.

The price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio measures a stock’s valuation by dividing its share price by its earnings per share.

The risks of investing in securities of foreign issuers, including emerging market issuers, can include fluctuations in foreign currencies, political and economic instability, and foreign taxation issues.

The Russell 1000® Value Index, a trademark/service mark of the Frank Russell Co.®, is an unmanaged index considered representative of large-cap value stocks.

The Russell Midcap® Index, a trademark/service mark of the Frank Russell Co.®, is an unmanaged index considered representative of mid-cap stocks.

The S&P 500® Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of the US stock market.

Stocks of medium-sized companies tend to be more vulnerable to adverse developments, may be more volatile, and may be illiquid or restricted as to resale.

In general, stock values fluctuate, sometimes widely, in response to activities specific to the company as well as general market, economic and political conditions.

A value style of investing is subject to the risk that the valuations never improve or that the returns will trail other styles of investing or the overall stock market.

The opinions referenced above are those of the author as of May 20, 2025. These comments should not be construed as recommendations, but as an illustration of broader themes. Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. They involve risks, uncertainties, and assumptions; there can be no assurance that actual results will not differ materially from expectations.

Above the Noise: Making sense of markets, Treasuries, and tariffs by Invesco US

data.title: Lazard Global Listed Infrastructure Portfolio Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Equity markets worldwide receded in the first quarter, as investors trod cautiously amid growing risks and mounting uncertainty about the global economic outlook. All eyes were on the US, where market enthusiasm for the administration’s anticipated business-friendly policies was tempered amid tariff announcements and speculation about their scope. The European Central Bank reduced interest rates at its policy meetings in January and March and noted that the “disinflation process remained on track” in the eurozone.

Portfolio Review

In the first quarter, the Lazard Global Listed Infrastructure Portfolio outperformed its benchmark, the MSCI World Core Infrastructure (USD Hedged) Index. (Return is measured net of fees and in US dollars.)

US diversified utility Exelon (EXC) (4.9% weighting in the Portfolio) posted gains after a strong set of fourth quarter results and its safe haven status in a turbulent market. The visibility of its regulated returns, combined with the attractiveness of its valuation, made the company a key focus for investors. Notably, the company raised its guidance for 2025. Exelon continues to strategically reallocate capital toward higher return Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) transmission investments, which are expected to drive long-term value creation and sustained earnings growth.

French toll road operator Vinci (OTCPK:VCISF) (4.6% weighting) delivered solid performance during the quarter, supported by robust traffic levels across its motorway network and airports, now standing around 8% above 2019 levels overall. Following a year marked by political uncertainty in France, the company’s shift in focus toward international opportunities is becoming evident. A notable example is Vinci’s 50%-owned Gatwick Airport in the UK, which received support from the UK government to advance plans for a second runway. This expansion would significantly increase the airport's capacity and, if executed effectively, we believe it could unlock substantial value.

Italian gas utility Snam (OTCPK:SNMRF) (6.9% weighting) rose during the quarter after unveiling an updated investment plan for 2025-2029. The plan highlights significant investments in transforming the network to accommodate hydrogen and biogas blending. Additionally, investments are directed at improving Europe’s security of supply, bringing greater flexibility to the network to adapt to changing gas flows. We believe that Snam offers a compelling opportunity at current prices, in light of its robust and consistent regulatory framework.

Sister listed infrastructure investment holding companies, Power Assets (PAH) (OTCPK:HGKGF) (3.5% weighting) and CK Infrastructure (CKI) (2.4% weighting), both experienced declines during the quarter, continuing a period of price volatility. Rumours have been circulating regarding the potential sale of UK Rail's business Eversholt Rail for a combined price of GBP 4 billion, which is a premium to the purchase price paid in 2015. Additionally, we understand both companies have interest in acquiring UK regulated water utility Thames Water. It is understood that CKI has made a preliminary bid for a majority stake in Thames Water, valued at £7 billion, contingent on Thames Water's bondholders agreeing to significant debt write-downs. We believe acquiring Thames Water at a discount to Regulatory Capital Value would be a value accretive transaction.

Shares in UK water utility United Utilities (UU) (OTCPK:UUGWF) (4.6% weighting) faced pressure during the quarter, driven by rising UK government gilt yields and broader weakness across the UK water sector as several peers appealed Ofwat’s regulatory decision issued in December 2024. Unlike its peers, UU accepted the regulator’s final determination of tariffs for the 2025-2030 regulatory period. We view this regulatory review positively, as it not only improved the allowed return, but also de-risked critical items such as power costs and the Output Delivery Incentives, leaving greater potential for outperformance over the next five years. We maintain a positive view on the company’s top quartile operational performance and its readiness for the significant capital programme ahead, positioning it as one of the most attractive investment opportunities within our universe.

Outlook

The combination of volatile equity markets and our conservative approach leads us to view current market conditions cautiously.

We see pockets of attractive value opportunities, particularly in Europe. Although inflation shows signs of moderating, we expect it will remain above most central bank target ranges for several years. High bursts of inflation have positive cash flow implications for toll roads, airports, railways and non-US utilities. In contrast, the implications of higher inflation for US utilities are likely negative.

We have long cautioned investors about the valuation of the US utility sector, and we have been underweight this sector for some time. While we remain cautious, we are beginning to see specific stock opportunities within the sector, which we may pursue in the months ahead.

The scarce valuation opportunities have led to a relatively concentrated portfolio where we believe the risk/return trade-off is favourable; however, this brings a higher degree of stock-specific risk. In our opinion, the only way to generate returns that properly compensate for the risk taken is through highly selective stock-picking. We caution investors to expect increased volatility in the short to medium term. Value is emerging now and on a 5-year view and valuations look more attractive on a risk/return basis. We believe returns available in the strategy look relatively attractive at this time when compared to a passive investment in infrastructure indices, bonds or in broader equity markets. We believe the preferred infrastructure characteristics we seek for all our investments will continue to serve our investors well over the longer term.
